skills.prompt: This topic introduces entropy for AP Chemistry students (CED: ENE-4.A, Unit 9, Topic 9.1). Students should understand that entropy (S) is a measure of the number of possible microstates (molecular arrangements) of a system, often described as a measure of disorder or energy dispersal. Entropy increases when: solids melt or liquids vaporize, gases expand, temperature increases, the number of particles increases (reactions producing more moles of gas), or complex molecules form from simpler ones. The second law of thermodynamics states that the entropy of the universe always increases for spontaneous processes. Students must predict the sign of delta-S for given processes and compare entropy of different states. Connect to Gibbs free energy, spontaneity, and thermodynamic favorability. AP exam note: predicting entropy changes qualitatively is commonly tested. Vocabulary: entropy, microstates, disorder, energy dispersal, second law, spontaneous, delta-S, positional entropy.

This topic covers absolute entropy and entropy change for AP Chemistry students (CED: ENE-4.B, Unit 9, Topic 9.2). Students should understand that the third law of thermodynamics states that the entropy of a perfect crystal at absolute zero (0 K) is zero. This allows measurement of absolute (standard) molar entropy values S-degrees for all substances. Entropy change for a reaction is calculated as: delta-S_rxn = sum of S-degrees(products) minus sum of S-degrees(reactants), weighted by stoichiometric coefficients. Unlike enthalpy of formation, absolute entropy values are never zero for elements. Larger, more complex molecules have higher absolute entropies. Students must calculate delta-S from standard entropy tables and predict signs. Connect to Gibbs free energy, spontaneity, and entropy concepts. AP exam note: calculating delta-S from standard entropy values is a tested quantitative skill. Vocabulary: absolute entropy, third law, standard molar entropy, delta-S, perfect crystal, stoichiometric calculation.

skills.skill: 9.3 Gibbs Free Energy and Thermodynamic Favorability
skills.prompt: This topic covers Gibbs free energy for AP Chemistry students (CED: ENE-4.C, Unit 9, Topic 9.3). Students should understand that Gibbs free energy change (delta-G = delta-H - T*delta-S) determines thermodynamic favorability (spontaneity). When delta-G < 0, the process is thermodynamically favorable (spontaneous). When delta-G > 0, it is unfavorable. When delta-G = 0, the system is at equilibrium. There are four enthalpy-entropy combinations: both favorable (always spontaneous), both unfavorable (never spontaneous), and two temperature-dependent cases. Delta-G-degrees can be calculated from delta-H-degrees and delta-S-degrees, or from standard free energies of formation. Students must calculate delta-G, determine favorability, and identify the temperature at which favorability changes (T = delta-H/delta-S). AP exam note: Gibbs free energy calculations and favorability predictions are heavily tested. Vocabulary: Gibbs free energy, thermodynamic favorability, spontaneous, nonspontaneous, delta-G, delta-H, delta-S, temperature dependence.

This topic covers thermodynamic and kinetic control for AP Chemistry students (CED: ENE-4.D, Unit 9, Topic 9.4). Students should understand the distinction between thermodynamic favorability and kinetic feasibility. A reaction can be thermodynamically favorable (delta-G < 0) but kinetically slow due to a high activation energy barrier. Diamond converting to graphite is thermodynamically favorable but kinetically controlled (extremely slow). Conversely, a thermodynamically unfavorable reaction will not proceed regardless of kinetics. Catalysts affect kinetics but not thermodynamics. Students must explain why some favorable reactions do not occur at observable rates and distinguish between thermodynamic and kinetic arguments. Connect to activation energy, catalysis, and Gibbs free energy. AP exam note: explaining why thermodynamically favorable reactions may not be observed is important. Vocabulary: thermodynamic control, kinetic control, activation energy, kinetic stability, thermodynamic stability, catalyst, spontaneity, rate.

This topic covers the relationship between free energy and equilibrium for AP Chemistry students (CED: ENE-5.A, Unit 9, Topic 9.5). Students should understand that delta-G-degrees and K are related by delta-G-degrees = -RT*ln(K). When delta-G-degrees < 0, K > 1 (products favored at equilibrium). When delta-G-degrees > 0, K < 1 (reactants favored). When delta-G-degrees = 0, K = 1. At non-standard conditions, delta-G = delta-G-degrees + RT*ln(Q). At equilibrium, delta-G = 0 (not delta-G-degrees). Students must calculate K from delta-G-degrees and vice versa, and explain the relationship between free energy and equilibrium position. Connect to equilibrium constants, Le Chatelier's principle, and thermodynamics. AP exam note: the delta-G and K relationship is a key conceptual and quantitative topic. Vocabulary: Gibbs free energy, equilibrium constant, delta-G-degrees, reaction quotient, standard conditions, non-standard conditions.

This topic covers free energy of dissolution for AP Chemistry students (CED: ENE-5.B, Unit 9, Topic 9.6). Students should understand that the dissolution of a salt involves both enthalpy and entropy changes. Lattice energy (energy to separate ions, endothermic) and hydration energy (energy released when ions interact with water, exothermic) determine delta-H of dissolution. Entropy generally increases upon dissolution (more dispersed ions and solvent molecules). Dissolution is thermodynamically favorable when delta-G < 0, which depends on the balance of delta-H and T*delta-S. Some endothermic dissolutions are spontaneous because the entropy increase overcomes the unfavorable enthalpy. Students must explain solubility in terms of free energy and the balance of enthalpic and entropic factors. Connect to Ksp, intermolecular forces, and lattice energy. AP exam note: explaining why some salts dissolve despite positive delta-H is tested. Vocabulary: dissolution, lattice energy, hydration energy, entropy of dissolution, free energy, Ksp, spontaneous dissolution.

This topic covers coupled reactions for AP Chemistry students (CED: ENE-5.C, Unit 9, Topic 9.7). Students should understand that a thermodynamically unfavorable reaction (positive delta-G) can be driven forward by coupling it with a strongly favorable reaction (very negative delta-G) so that the overall delta-G is negative. The most important biological example is ATP hydrolysis (delta-G approximately -30.5 kJ/mol) driving otherwise unfavorable biochemical reactions. In chemistry, coupling reactions is applied in extractive metallurgy and industrial synthesis. The overall delta-G equals the sum of individual delta-G values (analogous to Hess's law). Students must calculate overall delta-G for coupled reactions and determine whether the coupled process is favorable. Connect to Hess's law, Gibbs free energy, and biological energetics. AP exam note: coupled reaction problems test understanding of additivity of delta-G. Vocabulary: coupled reactions, thermodynamically unfavorable, driving force, ATP hydrolysis, overall delta-G, Hess's law, free energy additivity.

This topic covers galvanic and electrolytic cells for AP Chemistry students (CED: ENE-6.A, Unit 9, Topic 9.8). Students should understand that galvanic (voltaic) cells convert chemical energy to electrical energy spontaneously (delta-G < 0, positive E-cell). The anode is the site of oxidation (negative terminal), the cathode is the site of reduction (positive terminal), and a salt bridge maintains electrical neutrality. Electrolytic cells use external electrical energy to drive nonspontaneous reactions (delta-G > 0, negative E-cell). In electrolytic cells, the anode is positive and the cathode is negative (externally forced). Students must diagram both cell types, label components, identify anode/cathode, write half-reactions, and determine electron flow direction. Connect to redox reactions, cell potential, and Faraday's law. AP exam note: comparing galvanic and electrolytic cells is heavily tested. Vocabulary: galvanic cell, electrolytic cell, anode, cathode, salt bridge, half-reaction, oxidation, reduction, electrode, external circuit.

This topic covers cell potential and free energy for AP Chemistry students (CED: ENE-6.B, Unit 9, Topic 9.9). Students should understand that standard cell potential E-degrees-cell = E-degrees-cathode - E-degrees-anode, using standard reduction potentials from a reference table. A positive E-degrees-cell indicates a spontaneous reaction. The relationship between cell potential and free energy is delta-G-degrees = -nFE-degrees-cell, where n is moles of electrons transferred and F is Faraday's constant (96485 C/mol). This connects electrochemistry to thermodynamics. Students must calculate E-degrees-cell from half-reaction potentials, determine spontaneity, and convert between E-degrees, delta-G-degrees, and K. The relationship delta-G-degrees = -RT*ln(K) links all three quantities. AP exam note: calculating and interpreting cell potentials is a core skill. Vocabulary: standard cell potential, standard reduction potential, Faraday's constant, spontaneous, nonspontaneous, half-reaction, delta-G, equilibrium constant.

This topic covers cell potential under nonstandard conditions for AP Chemistry students (CED: ENE-6.C, Unit 9, Topic 9.10). Students should understand the Nernst equation: E = E-degrees - (RT/nF)*ln(Q), which at 25 degrees C simplifies to E = E-degrees - (0.0592/n)*log(Q). When Q < K, E is positive and the reaction proceeds forward. When Q > K, E is negative. At equilibrium, Q = K and E = 0. Changes in concentration affect cell potential: increasing reactant concentration or decreasing product concentration increases E. As a galvanic cell operates, concentrations change, Q approaches K, and E decreases to zero (cell is dead). Students must calculate E under nonstandard conditions and predict how concentration changes affect voltage. Connect to equilibrium, Le Chatelier's principle, and battery operation. AP exam note: Nernst equation calculations are tested. Vocabulary: Nernst equation, nonstandard conditions, reaction quotient Q, cell potential, concentration cell, equilibrium.

This topic covers electrolysis and Faraday’s law for AP Chemistry students (CED: ENE-6.D, Unit 9, Topic 9.11). Students should understand that electrolysis uses electrical energy to drive nonspontaneous reactions, such as electroplating metals, decomposing compounds, and producing elements (chlor-alkali process, aluminum smelting). Faraday’s law relates the amount of substance produced to the charge passed: moles of substance = (current x time) / (n x F), where current is in amperes, time in seconds, n is electrons per formula unit, and F = 96485 C/mol. Students must calculate the mass of substance deposited or produced given current and time, and determine the time or current needed for a desired amount. Connect to redox stoichiometry, cell potential, and industrial applications. AP exam note: Faraday’s law calculations are commonly tested. Vocabulary: electrolysis, Faraday’s law, coulombs, amperes, electroplating, current, charge, moles of electrons, stoichiometry.
